title: Area A Quiz: How Generative AI Works (10 questions, pass with 80% or higher)

questions.q: Which of the following best describes how generative AI (a large language model, or LLM) produces text?
choices: It probabilistically predicts and arranges the next word based on the preceding context
It converts learned text into a database and searches it for matching passages to return
It selects the closest match from a set of fixed response patterns prepared in advance by humans
It accesses the internet each time it is asked a question and simply summarizes the content of the latest page
questions.explanation: An LLM learns statistical patterns from a huge volume of pre-training text, and generates continuations by probabilistically selecting the 'most likely next word' given the input context (strictly speaking, it predicts in units called 'tokens', which are smaller than words). It does not convert what it learned into a database and search it for matching passages (option 2), nor does it select from a fixed set of response templates (option 3). More and more services do combine an LLM with a web-search feature, but even then the response is not simply a summary of the latest page returned as-is -- the LLM generates the text anew each time, using the search results as material (option 4).

questions.q: A resident entered the same question into a generative AI twice, opening a fresh conversation (chat) each time, and the second response came back somewhat different from the first. Which of the following best explains this?
choices: Because the first input triggered retraining of the underlying model, improving its performance by the second attempt
Because AI output involves a degree of probabilistic variability, so wording and details can vary
Because a sudden surge in traffic congested the server, forcing it to return a simplified, lower-quality answer
Because a built-in anti-plagiarism feature deliberately varies the content of the answer on every request
questions.explanation: Most generative AI systems don't simply choose the single highest-probability word each time; they build in a degree of variability (sampling). That's why the same input doesn't always produce exactly the same text. A single exchange does not retrain the underlying model or improve its performance (option 1), server congestion did not degrade the quality of the answer (option 3), and there is no anti-plagiarism feature that deliberately varies the content on every request (option 4). Note that if you re-ask within the same chat, the preceding exchange (the context) also influences the output, so both sampling and context can contribute to the difference.

questions.q: A colleague explained that "generative AI searches a huge medical literature database and returns the matching passage as a direct quotation." Which of the following is the most appropriate response to this explanation?
choices: It uses basically the same underlying technology as a web search engine, so as a description of the mechanism the explanation contains no major error
Generative AI holds no database of any kind internally, so the correct point is that it should be unable to answer any medical question in the first place
Rather than searching, the LLM itself always generates each word anew from learned patterns, and even when a search feature is used the response is not necessarily a quotation of an existing passage
It always provides the URL of the primary source it consulted with every answer, so the only real flaw in the explanation is the missing source citations
questions.explanation: The LLM itself does not search a literature database and quote the matching passage; based on patterns it acquired for plausible ways to continue a passage, it newly generates text each time. Many services now combine an LLM with a web-search feature, but even then the response text is generated on the spot and is not necessarily a verbatim quotation of any real, existing passage -- and misattributed citations or errors in summarizing and integrating the retrieved material can also occur. The claim that it uses basically the same technology as a search engine (option 1), the claim that it should be unable to answer any medical question at all (option 2), and the claim that it always provides a primary-source URL with every answer (option 4) are all inconsistent with how it actually works.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes the basic mechanism of image-generation AI (diffusion models)?
choices: Just like text-generation AI, it predicts pixels one at a time using a next-word-prediction mechanism
It converts existing images gathered during training into a database and displays a combination of the ones closest to the instructions
It builds a three-dimensional model internally and renders it as a two-dimensional image from a given angle
It starts from an image of pure random noise and gradually removes that noise, step by step, to restore the image
questions.explanation: A diffusion model works by starting from an image containing random noise and progressively removing that noise, restoring an image that approximates the given instructions. This differs from the word-by-word prediction mechanism of text-generation AI (option 1); it is not a matter of converting existing images into a database and combining them (option 2); nor does it involve building a 3D model internally (option 3). Note that diffusion models are one representative approach to image generation, and they can also start from an existing image, as in image editing, rather than from pure noise.

questions.q: A nursing student said, "Text-generation AI and image-generation AI that uses diffusion models work by the same mechanism." Which of the following is the most appropriate response to this statement?
choices: Text-generation AI predicts the next word, while a diffusion model restores an image from noise -- the underlying principles differ
The two merely differ in the type of training data they use in bulk; internally they share essentially the same process
A diffusion model requires less computation than text generation, so it is designed as the simpler mechanism of the two
Because LLMs are designed to handle strings of characters rather than images, comparing them to image-generation AI is not a valid comparison
questions.explanation: Text-generation AI (an LLM) works by probabilistically predicting the next word, while image-generation AI based on diffusion models works by gradually restoring an image from noise -- the two operate on different principles. (Some newer image-generation AI does produce images with the same 'predict what comes next' approach as an LLM, but that does not change the fact that diffusion models and LLMs rest on different principles.) The claim that they merely differ in training-data type while sharing essentially the same internal process (option 2), the claim that a diffusion model is simpler because it needs less computation (option 3), and the claim that comparing the two is not valid because LLMs only handle characters (option 4) all misrepresent how the two mechanisms actually differ.

questions.q: Which of the following, framed in terms of how LLMs work, best explains why generative AI can output factually incorrect information (hallucination) in a plausible-sounding way?
choices: An LLM alone, without a search function, does not look up correct information; it probabilistically generates plausible word sequences, so content that differs from fact can result
Because the AI's training data always lags several years behind, no matter the field, and that alone explains every one of the errors
Because developers deliberately build incorrect information into the answers in advance, as part of testing to evaluate the model's performance
Hallucination is a malfunction limited to early prototype models, and it hardly ever occurs in any of the current generative AI systems
questions.explanation: An LLM alone, without a search function, does not "search for and return correct information"; rather, it probabilistically generates a sequence of words that is contextually plausible. As a result, content that differs from fact (hallucination) can be generated plausibly as long as it is grammatically and contextually natural. In recent years, combining the model with search or external reference material (RAG) has become a common way to reduce hallucination, but misattributed citations and summarization errors remain possible, so verification is still needed. It is not because the training data always lags several years behind regardless of field (option 2), nor because developers deliberately build in incorrect information (option 3); and it is not a malfunction confined to early prototype models that hardly ever occurs today (option 4 is wrong).

questions.q: A resident asked a generative AI, with its web-search feature turned off, to suggest some references, and it presented a paper with specific author names, a journal name, and even a volume/issue number. However, that paper did not actually exist. Which of the following best explains this situation?
choices: The paper once existed but was retracted and removed from the databases, so it simply can no longer be confirmed today
Generative AI is directly linked to reference databases and always cross-checks against them, so a nonexistent reference could not possibly be shown
Because the AI has learned the formatting patterns of real papers, it can generate a nonexistent paper in a plausible format
Because the database being searched was temporarily down, a substitute reference was displayed automatically instead
questions.explanation: Generative AI has learned the formatting patterns of bibliographic information (author names, journal names, volume/issue numbers, etc.) for real papers, so it can generate bibliographic information in a plausible format in response to a request. As a result, a nonexistent paper can be presented as if it genuinely existed. The retraction explanation (option 1) does not fit the situation, because the paper never existed in the first place. The premise that AI is directly linked to reference databases and always cross-checks against them, so this could not happen, is false (option 2), and the explanation that a substitute reference was displayed because the database was down also does not match how the mechanism actually works (option 4). Note that turning on a web-search feature makes real references more likely, but even then a URL can lead to a different paper than claimed, so checking the actual source is always necessary.

questions.q: Which of the following situations is most likely to lead to hallucination?
choices: When asking about common terms that turn up throughout textbooks and the literature, such as "hypertension" or "diabetes"
When exchanging casual small talk that has nothing at all to do with medicine, such as greetings or brief comments about the local weather that day
Only on the very first question you happen to ask on the day you newly start using that particular AI account
When asking about matters that appear infrequently in the training data and are prone to being vague, such as minor proper nouns or specific figures
questions.explanation: Hallucination is especially likely to occur when you ask about matters prone to being vague -- minor proper nouns, specific figures, and other things that appear infrequently in the training data. It tends to be relatively stable for common terms that recur throughout textbooks and the literature (option 1) or for casual small talk unrelated to medicine (option 2), and it is not an effect limited to the very first question asked on the day you start using a given AI account (option 3).

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes what generative AI can create?
choices: It can only generate text; it is unable to produce images, audio, or program code of any kind whatsoever, according to this claim
Some generative AI can produce a variety of formats beyond text, including images, slide drafts, program code, and audio
It is designed exclusively for generating audio, and lacks any capability to generate text or program code whatsoever
It can generate video and still images, but lacks the capability to summarize a long passage of text
questions.explanation: Some generative AI systems can produce not only text but also images, slide outlines, program code, audio, and other varied formats (multimodal). Describing generative AI as text-only and unable to produce anything else (option 1), audio-only (option 3), or unable to summarize (option 4) all improperly generalize the characteristics of some generative AI systems to all of them, and none of these descriptions is appropriate.

questions.q: A nurse is considering how to use generative AI in their work. Which of the following is the most appropriate example?
choices: Entering a discharge summary as-is, including the patient's real name and chart number, to have the AI produce a summary
Using an AI-generated medical explanation directly in an explanation to a patient, without checking the content at all
Having the AI draft an in-hospital training document containing no patient information, then checking and revising the final content yourself
Letting the final decision on a patient's treatment plan proceed exactly as the generative AI proposed, without checking any of the content yourself
questions.explanation: Having the AI draft an in-hospital document containing no patient information, then personally checking and revising the final content before use, is an example of an appropriate professional use of generative AI. On the other hand, entering a patient's real name and chart number as-is (option 1), using AI-generated content directly for a patient explanation without checking it (option 2), and letting the final treatment-plan decision proceed exactly as the AI proposed without checking it (option 4) are all problematic from a safety and ethical standpoint.
